The Tumwater Brewery AGM

The offering

At the falls of the Deschutes River, the brewhouse of the Olympia Brewing Company has stood since 1906 — the landmark anchor of a riverfront assemblage now presented, off-market, to a selected group of qualified parties.

Site photograph — KPFF civil due diligence (2024).

Investment highlights

Why this opportunity merits attention.

An irreplaceable riverfront assemblage

Thirty-four acres at the falls of the Deschutes, anchored by the 1906 brewhouse — landmark fabric and a river-and-falls setting that cannot be newly constructed.

Diligence already in hand

A completed conceptual design (GCH, 2022–23), third-party civil due diligence (KPFF, 2024), and a market analysis (Concord Group) precede the offering.

Multiple exit paths

Resort and hotel, membership club, investor and family-office land basis, and a tribal-nation stewardship case — optionality preserved at disposition.

Public-sector tailwinds

A $500,000 EPA Brownfields grant supports area environmental assessment, and the State's removal of the 5th Avenue Dam is projected to lower the base flood elevation along the property's frontage.

An undersupplied market

No heritage boutique or destination-resort product operates in the South Sound competitive set.

Off-market

Presented confidentially to a selected group of qualified parties, not publicly listed.

Highest and best use

Four readings of the property, by acquirer.

The property has been evaluated against four acquirer profiles. Each page presents the program, market context, and economic framework in the terms that acquirer applies during initial review.

Examine the underwriting case

The vision, studied

Design and diligence precede the offering.

The property arrives with its principal pre-development work complete: a full conceptual design with site concepts and sections, third-party civil engineering due diligence, and a market analysis. The documents are available to qualified parties in the data room.

Concept D1 site plan and section — GCH (2023). Draft issue, as published.

Market analysis

Timing

Conditions converging at the property.

Estuary restoration

The State's removal of the 5th Avenue Dam will restore the Deschutes Estuary along the property's frontage. The final environmental impact statement projects a lower base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events.

Municipal investment

The City has completed pavement work on the Custer Way frontage and previously upgraded the sanitary lift station serving the site. The ongoing municipal pipeline is recorded under News & Developments.

Market opening

The competitive market holds no boutique or destination-resort lodging. The market study identifies supportable boutique demand at the site; the analysis appears with the resort program.

Overview

A destination hotel differentiated by place, not product type.

This is not a conventional hotel parcel. The former Olympia Brewery presents a hospitality opportunity with genuine competitive separation — a 1906 landmark setting at the falls of the Deschutes, immediate adjacency to Brewery Park at Tumwater Falls, and a studied program that extends well beyond guestrooms into events, wellness, food and beverage, and destination-oriented public space.

The opportunity is not simply to build keys. It is to establish the hospitality anchor within a place that already carries identity, public recognition, and completed conceptual work.

Differentiation

Why this site is different.

Most hotel sites compete on visibility, access, and replacement cost. This one competes on scarcity. The 1906 Old Brewhouse is Tumwater's best-known landmark, and the site combines historic structures, river-and-falls adjacency, and public-park context in a way that is difficult to replicate anywhere in the local market. That distinction does real commercial work: hospitality value is created by the experience, event setting, and brand narrative an asset can deliver — not by room count alone — and here the historic fabric and surrounding landscape contribute from the outset.

Historic identity

The Old Brewhouse dates to 1906 and sits within a recognized historic district — architectural legitimacy and brand depth that are hard to reproduce in new construction, and that support premium room categories.

Natural setting

Immediate adjacency to Brewery Park at Tumwater Falls gives guests, events, and public-facing uses an experiential context built in, strengthening leisure and event-market appeal.

District context

The property sits within a broader Brewery District redevelopment vision rather than as a stand-alone parcel — the hotel reads as embedded in a real place, not isolated on a private pad.

Program

A program with multiple revenue drivers.

A 2022–23 conceptual design study established a complete program across the 1906 brewhouse and four additional historic structures — a 72-key boutique hotel, event facilities, wellness, food and beverage, and structured parking — with floor plans, square footages, and circulation documented. Rooms can be the core of the business here, but they need not be the whole of it: the revenue thesis can draw on overnight demand, weddings and retreats, spa and day-use traffic, and food and beverage alike. Differentiated hospitality assets are typically underwritten on the quality and diversity of that revenue stack, not on stabilized occupancy alone.

72-key boutique hotel

Standard, premium, suite, and penthouse rooms within the five-story Cellar building. River-facing suites occupy the west end of each floor.

26,185 SF event center

A double-height event volume within the Warehouse building, supplemented by three outdoor venues and a waterfront plaza.

15,540 SF wellness center

Bathhouse, spa, salon, fitness, and café facilities within the Keg House, serving both hotel guests and a membership base.

Brewing and food & beverage

The six-story brew tower is illustrated for a public brewing and dining use, consistent with its historic function.

298-stall parking structure

Three parking levels recessed into the southern hillside, with grade-separated entries and tunnel connections to the principal buildings.

Vertical arrival sequence

A skybridge or escalator descent of approximately 110 vertical feet through forested hillside, connecting upper parking to the waterfront level.

Brew Tower

The six-story 1906 landmark, programmed for public brewing and dining. City-owned outparcel, coordinated with the program.

Hospitality — Cellar Building

The 72-key boutique hotel, with river-facing suites at the west end of each floor.

Event Center — Warehouse

26,185 square feet of double-height event volume, with three outdoor venues nearby.

Wellness — Keg House

15,540 square feet of bathhouse, spa, salon, fitness, and café program.

Residential Overlay

Upland zones along the rail corridor, studied at 10–30 units per acre.

Pedestrian Bridge

River crossing connecting the waterfront plaza to the west-bank trail network.

Select a marker to read the program at each location. Plan: Concept D1 — GCH (2023).

Market context

A market gap, not just a market entry.

This is better understood as a white-space play than as another hotel added to an existing inventory set. The competitive market area holds approximately 1,700 hotel rooms — roughly 70 percent of Thurston County's inventory — with an average property age near twenty years and a composition concentrated in midscale and economy product. No heritage boutique or destination-resort property operates in the market. Demand is anchored by state government: market occupancy has run near 64 percent, with government-related business representing approximately 70 percent of occupancy during the legislative quarter.

~100
boutique rooms identified as supportable at the site (Concord Group)
~1,700
rooms in the competitive market area; no boutique comparable
64%
market occupancy, with seasonal government-driven peaks

Market figures derive from the Concord Group study (2014 base year) and should be refreshed with current STR / CoStar data during diligence.

Regional destination supply

The destination resorts of western Washington, mapped.

Every comparable destination property in the region sits east into the mountains, west on Hood Canal, north at the border, or south in the Columbia Gorge. None operates in the South Sound, and none occupies a pre-war industrial landmark. The site sits on the I-5 corridor between all of them.

PropertyKeysSettingOriginDrive (approx.)
The Tumwater Brewery (subject)72 studiedDeschutes riverfront, at the falls1906 landmark structuresSeattle 1 hr · Portland 1¾ hr
Salish Lodge & Spa — Snoqualmie86Snoqualmie FallsRebuilt 1988 (1916 inn site)Seattle 30 min
Suncadia — Cle Elum≈ 250 + 18 (Inn)Cascade foothills, Cle Elum RiverBuilt 2000sSeattle 1½ hr
Alderbrook Resort & Spa — Union77 + 16 cottagesHood Canal waterfrontRebuilt 2004 (est. 1913)Seattle 2 hr
Semiahmoo Resort — Blaine196Semiahmoo Bay spitBuilt 1987 (cannery site)Seattle 2 hr
Skamania Lodge — Stevenson254 + treehousesColumbia River GorgeBuilt 1993Portland 45 min

Key counts and amenities per property and operator publications, reviewed June 2026. Drive times approximate. Subject-site figures per the GCH conceptual design.

Economic considerations

Revenue and valuation framework.

  • Rate positioning. A heritage boutique property would occupy a segment above the existing comparable set, which consists primarily of dated midscale product.
  • Diversified revenue. The program combines rooms, event and wedding facilities, wellness memberships, food and beverage, and retail; the conceptual design notes these uses reinforce one another's utilization.
  • Asset scarcity. Riverfront acreage with a landmark structure adjacent to a planned estuary restoration is not reproducible elsewhere in this corridor.
  • Phasing capacity. Land and entitlement may be secured ahead of vertical investment, allowing capital deployment to follow demand.

Why now

The redevelopment story already carries public-sector recognition.

This is not an unframed land vision. The surrounding former-brewery area sits within a Brewery District redevelopment context that has already drawn public attention and assessment funding, while along the property's own frontage the State's Deschutes Estuary restoration advances toward dam removal. None of this removes the work ahead — it means the pathway is becoming more legible.

  • District momentum. A Brewery District redevelopment vision for the former brewery area has been publicly advanced, giving the subject site a context rather than an isolated pad.
  • Public funding at work. In 2023 the City of Tumwater was awarded a $500,000 EPA Brownfields grant to assess environmental conditions across the brewery properties and support cleanup planning.
  • Improving site condition. Removal of the 5th Avenue Dam is projected to lower the base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events along the frontage the property holds.

EPA Brownfields award and Brewery District planning per City of Tumwater and EPA announcements (2023). Estuary projection per the WA DES Deschutes Estuary Final EIS (2022).

Development reality

Complexity is part of the opportunity.

This is not a prototype-hotel parcel, and it should not be evaluated as one. The documented redevelopment conditions — environmental investigation, utility and fire-flow upgrades, and river- and flood-related constraints — are part of the broader site-planning picture, and they are detailed in the third-party civil due diligence on the Property page. Barriers of this kind tend to preserve differentiation: they are part of what keeps the outcome less interchangeable for the right developer.

  • Environmental. A documented soil and groundwater condition (a 2002 voluntary cleanup reached industrial levels) makes Phase II scoping a standard diligence item — now supported by the City's Brownfields assessment work.
  • Infrastructure. Utility and fire-flow planning, including a looped 8-inch water main for fire flow, is part of execution rather than an afterthought.
  • River and flood. FEMA Zone AE conditions on part of the site should be treated as design and entitlement matters from the outset; the estuary restoration is projected to ease them over the hold period.

Best-fit developer

Who this site is best suited for.

The strongest fit is a developer or operator that knows how to turn identity into revenue — a team that treats adaptive reuse, event programming, food and beverage, and wellness as meaningful drivers of return rather than secondary features. It is less compelling for a purely standardized lodging strategy — the best outcome will come from a team that builds around the site's character, public setting, and mixed-use logic.

Boutique & soft-brand hospitality

Developers positioning above the commodity flag set, where heritage and setting support pricing power.

Adaptive-reuse sponsors

Teams experienced in entitlement coordination and projects where character matters as much as efficiency.

Event-oriented operators

Groups that underwrite weddings, retreats, and private gatherings as a core demand source.

Wellness-integrated concepts

Operators treating spa, fitness, and day-use as part of the guest and visitor experience.

Next step

What you are evaluating, and how to begin.

In short: whether a rare historic site in Tumwater can become a hospitality asset with regional draw and durable identity, and whether that differentiation justifies the added complexity of redeveloping a legacy industrial property. Initial discussions focus on operating model, adaptive-reuse approach, entitlement path, and basis. The indicative sequence below follows.

Diligence on the existing studies

The conceptual design and civil due diligence provide a documented starting point; market, structural, and cost analyses would be updated.

City of Tumwater pre-application

The access-road section and adaptive-reuse pathway are matters for early coordination with the City.

Operator alignment

The program was reviewed with a regional hospitality consultancy during the design study; operator selection would follow acquisition.

Phased development

Hotel, event, and wellness components may be staged against demand and the estuary-restoration timeline.

Overview

A setting consistent with heritage membership-club portfolios.

Operators in the membership and lifestyle-club category typically seek buildings of established character in settings their members regard as distinctive. The former Olympia Brewery offers 1906 masonry construction, river frontage, a forested hillside, and proximity to the State Capitol's professional calendar. The 2022 conceptual design organizes the property as an integrated clubhouse, guest-room, and wellness program.

Program

Clubhouse, rooms, and wellness within the existing structures.

Clubhouse facilities

The Cellar and Warehouse buildings accommodate lounge, dining, event, and retail uses around a pedestrian waterfront courtyard.

Guest rooms

Seventy-two studied rooms, including river-facing suites and penthouse units, provide the lodging component of a club program.

Wellness facility

The Keg House is programmed for spa, sauna, salon, fitness, and café uses — the wellness anchor common to contemporary club models.

Riverfront and outdoor program

Kayak and water access, trail networks, and the adjacent estuary restoration support an extensive outdoor and recreational program.

Market context

Comparable brewery conversions.

The redevelopment of former breweries into mixed-use destinations is an established pattern in the United States. Comparable conversions combined residential and lodging uses with food, beverage, and cultural program, and several recorded rental and value premiums relative to their local markets.

Comparable projectProgramReported premium
Pearl Brewery — San Antonio, TX~293 residences, 146 hotel rooms20–82%
Pabst Brewing Co. — Milwaukee, WI~370 residences, 90 roomsNot reported
The Factory at Franklin — Franklin, TNRetail, office, cultural−8% to 10%
Landmark — New Braunfels, TX~290 residences, civic and retail27–59%

Comparable set and reported premiums per the Concord Group market study.

Portfolio considerations

Characteristics relevant to a club operator's evaluation.

  • Building character. Landmark 1906 construction of this scale is typically acquired rather than replicated; the property's fabric is itself the differentiator.
  • Consolidated program. Clubhouse, rooms, wellness, dining, and outdoor amenities are accommodated on a single site rather than assembled across multiple properties.
  • Regional catchment. The property draws on the Capitol's professional community and sits on the I-5 corridor between Seattle and Portland.

Overview

Riverfront land adjacent to the Deschutes Estuary restoration.

The Washington Department of Enterprise Services plans to remove the 5th Avenue Dam and restore approximately 260 acres of the Deschutes Estuary, reintroducing tidal exchange where Capitol Lake stands today. The property fronts the estuary's South Basin. For a tribal nation, the site presents an opportunity for stewardship, cultural interpretation, and sovereign development on landmark riverfront acreage.

Considerations

Three areas of alignment.

Jurisdictional framework

A sovereign development path may reduce the entitlement timeline and regulatory exposure that apply to conventional development on a critical-areas site.

Ecological alignment

The restoration converts freshwater wetlands to estuarine habitat of higher assessed ecological value, consistent with long-term stewardship objectives on the riverfront.

Heritage and program

The 1906 brewhouse and 34 acres can support a program combining cultural, ecological, and enterprise uses, including hospitality and gathering facilities.

The restoration

Documented effects at the South Basin.

260
acres of estuary restored upon dam removal
South
Basin adjacency; river-influenced, with no planned dredging
Lower
base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events, per the EIS

The environmental impact statement found the South Basin to be more river-influenced than tidal, with limited exposed tideflat at low water and minimal change to erosion and deposition patterns. Freshwater wetlands transition to estuarine wetlands, and a habitat-enhancement plan would govern invasive-species management.

Path forward

Anticipated sequence.

Confidential introduction

Review of the strategic case, the site record, and the estuary context under a confidentiality agreement.

Jurisdiction and stewardship framework

Definition of the development and trust pathway appropriate to the nation's objectives.

Program definition

Alignment of the reuse program — cultural, ecological, hospitality, enterprise — with the site's heritage and the nation's priorities.

Overview

A land acquisition with documented constraints and a phased path.

The offering comprises 34 acres of riverfront land with a landmark structure, in a jurisdiction with a stated interest in the property's redevelopment. A conceptual design study and third-party civil due diligence are complete: the constraints are documented, the entitlement path is identified, and the capital plan can be staged rather than committed at acquisition.

The assemblage

Four parcels.

ParcelAddressAcres
A · 45700700001240 Custer Way SW1.253
B · 781003000003233 Boston St SW32.067
C West · 45701200000—0.464
C East · 45701100000—0.398
Total assemblage34.182 ac

The historic brew-tower outparcel within Parcel B is owned by the City of Tumwater and is excluded from the assemblage.

Site constraints

Documented conditions and their treatment.

Per KPFF civil due diligence (2024)

The following conditions are documented in the third-party civil study, together with the treatment each indicates.

Access roadSubject to negotiation

Boston St SE descends approximately 110 vertical feet at grades of 8–15 percent. The study finds the existing alignment can meet fire-apparatus standards if widened; the road section is a matter for negotiation with the City.

Flood (FEMA Zone AE)Design condition

Portions of Parcel B lie within the 1-percent-annual-chance flood zone (base flood elevation 15 feet; flood-protection elevation 16 feet). The study identifies elevated and crawl-space construction as an established treatment, citing the Snoqualmie City Hall precedent.

Riparian and wetland buffersDelineation required

The Deschutes is a fish-bearing Type F stream carrying a 250-foot riparian buffer, reducible to 150 feet in exchange for habitat restoration. Wetland buffers of 50–100 feet are assumed pending categorization by a licensed biologist.

Steep slopesSetback applies

Eastern slopes exceed 40 percent (approaching 70 percent in places) and carry landslide-hazard buffers, reducible with geotechnical justification.

Environmental conditionFurther study required

Soil and groundwater contamination (arsenic and priority metals) is documented; a 2002 voluntary cleanup achieved industrial cleanup levels. A 2018 fire and a 2019 transformer release warrant additional investigation.

UtilitiesServiceable

Water service exists on site (an upgrade from 6-inch to a looped 8-inch main is required for fire flow); sanitary service is provided through an upgraded lift station to the LOTT system; gas is extendable by PSE; three-phase power is present.

Dam removalFavorable

The estuary restoration is projected to lower the base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events, improving a principal site constraint over the hold period.

Sequencing

The development clock against the external clock.

The transaction track below is indicative, not a schedule. The external track follows public agency milestones. Their interaction is the point: a principal site constraint — base flood elevation in extreme river floods — is projected to improve as the estuary is established.

Design & permitting

Dam removal

5th Avenue Dam removal and restoration construction, per the DES schedule.

Estuary established

Projected lower base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events (EIS).

Valuation framework

Considerations for underwriting.

  • Effective buildable area. Critical-area buffers constrain the developable envelope; the civil study identifies the effective buildable area, and the conceptual design documents an approximately 192,000-square-foot program within it.
  • Entitlement as the value event. The spread between land basis and entitled, program-ready land represents the principal return; the work required to achieve it is identified in the existing studies.
  • Staged capital. Land and entitlement may be secured first, with infrastructure and vertical investment staged against demand and the estuary timeline.
  • Multiple exit paths. Resort, membership-club, residential-overlay (approximately 43–130 units across two studied zones), and tribal pathways preserve optionality at disposition.

Origins

Established on the falls of the Deschutes, 1906.

The Olympia Brewing Company constructed its brewhouse at the falls of the Deschutes River in 1905–1906, drawing on the artesian water the company's advertising made famous — “It's the Water.” The lower falls of the Deschutes drop beside the property itself. The slogan and the Olympia marks remain the property of their current owner; the water, the falls, and the buildings remain with the site. Over the following century the brewery grew into one of the Pacific Northwest's recognized industrial landmarks. Its masonry buildings remain on the 34-acre riverfront site — structures of a scale and construction quality that contemporary development does not reproduce.

Archival photograph · Olympia Brewing Co.
To be licensed — Olympia Tumwater Foundation / Washington State Archives

Now: site photograph, KPFF (2024). Proposed: Concept D1, GCH (2023). Archival image pending licensing.

Existing structures

The five buildings and their studied uses.

BuildingScaleApprox. areaStudied reuse

Cellar Building5 stories~37,500 SF72-key boutique hotel

Building inventory per the GCH Olympia Brewery Conceptual Design Report. Combined existing gross floor area exceeds 100,000 square feet.

The role of heritage in the offering

The landmark status of the existing structures shapes both the development program and the entitlement path. It also constitutes the property's principal differentiation: each prospective use — resort, club, cultural, or mixed — rests on building fabric that cannot be newly constructed.

Site systems

Access, utilities, river, and overlay.

Access and circulation

The property is served from Custer Way, an arterial collector at the I-5 interchange, via Boston St SE, which descends approximately 110 vertical feet at grades of 8–15 percent. The civil study finds the existing alignment can satisfy fire-apparatus standards if widened; the road section is subject to negotiation with the City. A 298-stall parking structure is studied into the hillside.

Utilities

Water service exists on site (6-inch line at over 100 PSI; an upgrade to a looped 8-inch main is required for a 2,000 GPM fire flow). Sanitary service is provided through an upgraded lift station to the LOTT Clean Water Alliance system. PSE gas is extendable to the site, and three-phase power is present; undergrounding would be evaluated against road-corridor costs.

River and estuary

The Deschutes River bounds the property to the north and west; the site fronts the future Deschutes Estuary South Basin. Removal of the 5th Avenue Dam restores approximately 260 acres of estuary and is projected to lower the base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events. A gated maintenance road descends from the access drive to the falls reach of the river.

Residential overlay

Two upland residential zones are identified along the rail corridor: Zone 1 (approximately 1.75 acres, 5–10 percent slopes, 17–52 units) and Zone 2 (approximately 2.6 acres, 10–25 percent slopes, two tiers, 26–78 units) at densities of 10–30 units per acre, with a third zone identified near the Schmidt House.

Critical areas

Regulated conditions on the property.

Critical areaConditionStatus
FEMA flood zone (AE)BFE 15 ft; flood-protection elevation 16 ftDesign condition
Riparian habitat (Type F stream)250-ft buffer; reducible to 150 ft with restorationBuffer applies
Freshwater wetlands50–100 ft buffer pending categorizationDelineation required
Steep / landslide-hazard slopesEast slopes 40–70%Setback applies
Soil / groundwater contaminationArsenic, metals; 2002 cleanup to industrial levelsFurther study
Critical aquifer recharge / high groundwaterAlong river banksNoted

Source: KPFF civil due diligence (draft, June 2024). Full attachments are available in the confidential data room.

Plans and exhibits

Source drawings.

Anticipated questions

Matters most parties raise first.

Is the brew tower included in the assemblage?

No. The historic brew-tower outparcel within Parcel B is owned by the City of Tumwater and is excluded from the 34.182-acre total. The conceptual design programs it for public brewing and dining use in coordination with the City — a relationship most parties will want to define early in entitlement discussions.

What is the environmental exposure?

Soil and groundwater contamination (arsenic and priority metals) is documented, attributed to the former paint-shop area. A 2002 voluntary cleanup achieved industrial cleanup levels. A 2018 fire and a 2019 transformer release warrant additional investigation; defining a Phase II scope is a standard diligence item, and the agency records are available in the data room.

Can the access road support development?

The civil study finds the existing Boston St SE alignment can satisfy fire-apparatus standards if widened, and presents preliminary 20- and 30-foot road sections. The final section is a matter for negotiation with the City; the study recommends that meeting early in the process.

How is flood risk addressed?

Portions of Parcel B lie in FEMA Zone AE (base flood elevation 15 feet; flood-protection elevation 16 feet). The study identifies elevated and crawl-space construction as the established treatment, citing the Snoqualmie City Hall precedent — and the estuary restoration is projected to lower the base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events.

What is the process from here?

Confidentiality agreement, then data-room access, then a site visit, then indications of interest. The selling principal coordinates each step directly; begin with the request form.

About this record: a scheduled research routine reviews the City of Tumwater's permits, capital-improvement program, transportation projects, utility upgrades, and economic-development initiatives, and appends dated, sourced entries. Entries below are anchored to source documents; subsequent updates add current municipal items.

Oct 2022

Deschutes Estuary restoration advances toward dam removal

The Washington Department of Enterprise Services finalized the environmental impact statement for the Capitol Lake–Deschutes Estuary, selecting an estuary alternative that removes the 5th Avenue Dam and restores approximately 260 acres. The EIS indicates dam removal would lower the base flood elevation in extreme river-flood events at the South Basin, which the property fronts.

Source: WA DES, Capitol Lake–Deschutes Estuary Final EIS (October 2022).
2024

Custer Way identified for paving and preservation work

The City's Transportation and Engineering Department identified Custer Way — the property's arterial frontage — as a priority street for major repairs. Pavement upgrades appear to have been completed at the project frontage as of the June 2024 site walk.

Source: KPFF civil due diligence (2024), citing City of Tumwater.
2008

On-site sewer lift station upgraded under a City capital project

A 2008 City capital improvement upgraded the sanitary lift station serving the property and installed a force main beneath the Deschutes River to a second station in Tumwater Historical Park, establishing a sewer path with stated capacity for redevelopment flows.

Source: City of Tumwater Public Works as-built plans (2008), via KPFF.
Pending

City of Tumwater permits, capital program, and district planning

Reserved for scheduled updates: current Tumwater permit activity, capital-improvement and transportation projects, utility upgrades, and economic-development initiatives, including any Craft District planning — presented for a developer's and an investor's assessment of the regulatory and financial environment.

Updated on a recurring schedule by the research routine.

Exclusive representation

Exclusively represented by Troy Gessel.

The former Olympia Brewery is offered off-market through AGM Real Estate Group. Troy Gessel, Selling Principal, is the single point of contact for the offering — all materials, data-room access, and site visits are coordinated directly through him.

Process

How this offering works.

Information is released in stages as parties qualify. The sequence below is the standard path from first contact to evaluation; AGM will communicate any specific process or timing guidance directly.

1 · Confidentiality agreement

Qualified parties execute a confidentiality agreement. The offering is pre-market and is not publicly listed.

2 · Offering materials & data room

On execution, AGM provides the materials relevant to your interest — conceptual program, market analysis, and the KPFF civil due diligence — and access to the confidential data room.

3 · Site visit

Tours of the property and the existing structures are arranged for parties operating under confidentiality.

4 · Expression of interest

Interested parties submit an expression of interest or a letter of intent outlining proposed terms, basis, and intended use.

Request information

Request the offering materials.

Your request has been received. AGM Real Estate Group will respond with materials appropriate to your stated interest.

Materials are shared under a confidentiality agreement. This site is not indexed.
